title: Summit Fallout: US-Iran Escalation Swallows NATO Afterglow as Trump Departs Under Security Cloud
subhead: Within 48 hours of the Ankara Summit closing with pledges of unity, Trump declared the Iran ceasefire 'over' and launched strikes on 90 Iranian targets, Iran fired missiles at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain, and Trump switched Air Force One mid-flight citing security concerns — while Turkey locked in defense deals, satellite investments, and a pipeline extension with Iraq.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Collapses Amid New Strikes, Escalation Overshadows Summit
sections.body:
Within hours of the NATO Ankara Summit closing with declarations of transatlantic unity, the US-Iran conflict sharply escalated. President Trump declared the Iran ceasefire 'over' on July 8, and US Central Command announced it had struck approximately 90 Iranian military targets in a second night of operations on July 8-9, targeting sites along Iran's coastline including Sirik, Qeshm, and Bandar Abbas.

Iran's military responded by firing missiles and drones at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain, according to Reuters and Al Jazeera. The Guardian reported attacks on three locations across Iran after three commercial tankers were targeted in the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday. Qatar halted rapid LNG production after a tanker attack in Hormuz, according to Financial Express.

Trump departed Turkey aboard the older VC-25 Air Force One rather than the newly refurbished Qatari-gifted aircraft, switching planes at RAF Mildenhall in Britain — a move multiple outlets, including NBC News and AP, linked to security concerns. Retired Secret Service agents cited the decision as evidence of elevated threat assessments around Iran's capacity to target presidential transport.

The NATO Summit Declaration, issued just before the escalation, called on Iran to 'fully respect freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz' and reiterated that 'Iran must never have a nuclear weapon' — language that now reads as a prelude to the military campaign that followed.
sections.whyItMatters: The collapse of the Iran ceasefire days after NATO leaders celebrated unity in Ankara underscores how quickly summit diplomacy can be overtaken by military escalation. Turkey, which had positioned itself as a mediator in the US-Iran conflict, now faces a war on its southern flank that directly affects its energy imports, trade routes, and refugee calculus.

sections.heading: NATO Summit Declaration Confirms €70B Ukraine Pledge, $50B in Defense Deals
sections.body:
The Ankara Summit Declaration, released July 8, confirmed more than $50 billion in new NATO procurements and a pledge of €70 billion in military equipment, assistance, and training for Ukraine in 2026, with allies affirming commitments to 'at least equivalent levels in 2027.' The declaration also welcomed the EU's decision to provide multi-year funding through the Ukraine Support Loan.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said member states had spent $37 billion in one year on defense and called for a defense industry 'revolution.' Only five of 32 members are projected to meet the 3.5% core defense GDP target in 2026: Lithuania (5.33%), Estonia (5.1%), Latvia (4.92%), Poland (4.68%), and Greece (3.65%). Albania (1.48%), Slovenia (1.57%), and Czechia (1.86%) remain below even the old 2% floor, Reuters reported.

The summit also launched the NATO Drone Edge initiative, a $40 billion investment in counter-drone capabilities over five years, acknowledging that drones have 'fundamentally altered the character of modern warfare.' NATO committed to increasing military drone operators fivefold by end of 2027 and expanding flight training to 20 allied nations across 16 training centres.

On the industrial side, major deals included: formal negotiations for up to 10 Swedish Saab GlobalEye early-warning aircraft ($4.5B); a US-Germany MOU to produce ATACMS missiles in Germany; a letter of intent from Denmark, Finland, Germany, and Norway for MQ-4C Triton drones; and Finland's accession to the A330 MRTT tanker program.
sections.whyItMatters: The $50B procurement figure and the $40B drone initiative are the Alliance's largest single-summit commitments in years. Turkey's role as host gave it visibility and positioning — but the spending gap between frontline and southern/western members remains stark.

sections.heading: Turkey Locks In Defense Deals: ATMACA, Steel Dome Satellites, Precision Strike Consortium
sections.body:
Turkey used the summit's Defence Industrial Forum to announce several major investments. Roketsan will supply a significant quantity of long-range ATMACA land-based cruise missiles as part of a $1.6 billion package covering strike capabilities and ammunition, Defence24 reported. Turkey also joined the international Ground-Based Precision Strike Capabilities project alongside Denmark, France, Italy, Norway, and the UK.

The Turkish Armed Forces announced over $350 million in low-orbit satellites, military communications systems, and ASELSAN radars as part of the integrated 'Steel Dome' defense system, plus $300 million for two high-resolution observation satellites developed by TÜBİTAK. Turkey also joined the NATO HALO space initiative (Hybrid Alliance Layered Operations in Space) alongside Denmark, Canada, Finland, Germany, Norway, the Netherlands, and Sweden.

Turkey joined seven countries in an integrated air and missile defence initiative targeting low-altitude threats including drones and cruise missiles, and remained part of the seven-nation A400M transport fleet consortium with Belgium, Croatia, France, Poland, Spain, and the UK.

Roketsan and Havelsan both told the Financial Times (syndicated via MSN) that they expect growing demand from NATO and European countries to translate into new partnerships and technology-sharing agreements.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is converting its host-nation advantage into concrete defense industrial gains — ATMACA exports, satellite investments, and entry into multinational precision-strike and space consortia. These are not just purchases; they position Turkish firms inside NATO's supply chain for the next decade.

sections.heading: Trump Pledges CAATSA Lift, S-400 Transfer to Gulf Under Discussion
sections.body:
President Trump announced at the summit that the US 'will lift CAATSA sanctions' on Turkey, removing penalties imposed over Ankara's 2019 purchase of Russian S-400 air defense systems. However, the path to Turkey's return to the F-35 program remains complicated.

According to Hurriyet columnist Hande Firat, Turkey and the US are discussing transferring the S-400s to a Persian Gulf country — with Turkish media naming the UAE as a likely buyer. Physical removal of the S-400s from Turkish territory appears to be the formula Washington requires, though alternatives like deactivation, dismantling, or monitored storage were also discussed.

Even a CAATSA lift won't automatically restore F-35 access. Congress embedded separate provisions in the FY2021 NDAA restricting F-35 deliveries to Turkey, meaning the White House will need to convince Congress to amend those restrictions — a process Israel and Greece will lobby hard against. News18 and Armenian Weekly reported that Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu is 'furious' about the prospect of Turkey receiving F-35s.

Trump said he promised Erdogan 'five' F-35s, though on the F-35 he was more cautious, saying an assessment would be conducted and no decision had yet been reached. Separately, the State Department has already notified Congress of a planned $700 million sale of GE F110 engines for Turkey's indigenous KAAN fighter jet.
sections.whyItMatters: The CAATSA lift and S-400-to-Gulf transfer are the most significant US-Turkey defense breakthrough in years — but F-35 delivery requires a congressional fight that Israel and Greece will oppose. The $700M KAAN engine sale, already notified to Congress, is moving forward regardless.

sections.heading: Syria's New Parliament Excludes Kurdish PYD/SDF Representatives
sections.body:
Syria's interim parliament, sworn in after President Ahmed al-Sharaa appointed its last 70 members, contains no representatives from the PYD or SDF — the organizations that have led Kurdish governance in northeast Syria for over a decade. Instead, Sharaa pivoted toward the Kurdish National Council (KNC), an umbrella organization aligned with the Iraqi Kurdistan Democratic Party, Al Hurra reported.

The exclusion of the PYD/SDF from the interim legislature signals that Sharaa's government does not intend to share governing authority with the forces that control northeast Syria's ground reality. For Turkey, this is a welcome development: Ankara has long pressured Damascus and Washington to marginalize the PYD, which it considers a PKK affiliate.

However, analysts note that the KNC's legitimacy among Syrian Kurds is limited. Most Syrian Kurds distrust the new state after its attacks on Kurdish communities earlier this year, and many are critical of both PYD authoritarianism and KNC opportunism. The parliament's composition risks deepening rather than resolving the Kurdish question in Syria.
sections.whyItMatters: The exclusion of PYD/SDF from Syria's interim parliament aligns with Turkey's strategic preference — but creates a governance gap in northeast Syria where the SDF remains the de facto authority on the ground. A political settlement that excludes the dominant military force is unlikely to hold.

sections.heading: Turkey-Iraq Extend Kirkuk-Ceyhan Pipeline for 12 Months
sections.body:
Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar announced that Turkey and Iraq will sign a 12-month extension of the agreement governing the Iraq-Turkey Crude Oil Pipeline, preventing a July 27 expiration that would have cut off one of Baghdad's few remaining viable export routes.

The pipeline, running from Kirkuk to the Mediterranean terminal at Ceyhan, has become indispensable since the Strait of Hormuz was effectively shut down during the Iran war. With Iraq's southern exports trapped in the Gulf, the Ceyhan route transformed from a secondary option into an economic lifeline — oil accounts for roughly 90% of Iraqi government revenue, according to Reuters and OilPrice.

The pipeline has a turbulent history: it was offline for over two years after an arbitration court ordered Turkey to pay Baghdad $1.5 billion over unauthorized Kurdish oil exports between 2014 and 2018. Flows only resumed late last year. The one-year extension postpones but does not resolve the longer-term uncertainty around Iraq's export infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ceyhan pipeline extension secures Iraq's northern export route — and Turkey's leverage over it — for another year. As long as Hormuz remains disrupted, Turkey holds a critical chokepoint in global oil logistics.

sections.heading: Economy: TL Deposits Hit 11-Year High as Dedollarization Accelerates
sections.body:
Finance Minister Mehmet Simsek announced that the share of Turkish lira deposits in the banking sector has reached 62%, the highest level in 11 years. Central Bank data showed total banking sector deposits declining by 261.9 billion lira in the week ending July 3, while FX deposits fell by $3.25 billion — suggesting a continued shift from foreign currency into lira-denominated accounts.

The Central Bank's total reserves increased by $10.5 billion in the same period, indicating continued reserve accumulation. Banking sector loan volume surpassed 26 trillion lira.

Simsek characterized the deposit shift as evidence of economic program resilience 'despite multiple shocks' — a reference to the Iran war, Hormuz disruptions, and global market turbulence. Daily Sabah reported the figures.
sections.whyItMatters: The 62% TL deposit share is a tangible metric of Simsek's program gaining traction — dedollarization was a core policy goal. But with the Iran war disrupting energy prices and the lira under structural pressure, the question is whether this shift holds once external shocks intensify.

bottomLine: The 2026 NATO Ankara Summit was supposed to be Turkey's showcase — and in many ways it was, with $50B in Alliance procurements, a CAATSA lift pledge, and Turkish defense firms landing new NATO contracts. But within 48 hours, the Iran ceasefire collapsed, Trump launched strikes on 90 Iranian targets, and the US president departed Turkey on an older Air Force One due to security concerns. The summit's afterglow was swallowed by escalation. Turkey now faces a war on its southern flank that threatens energy imports, trade routes, and refugee flows — the same factors it spent weeks positioning itself to manage. Domestically, the dedollarization trend (62% TL deposits) and the Ceyhan pipeline extension offer Simsek and Bayraktar positive data points, but both depend on conditions that the Iran escalation could reverse. Meanwhile, Sharaa's exclusion of the PYD/SDF from Syria's interim parliament gives Turkey a diplomatic win in Damascus even as it deepens the governance vacuum in northeast Syria.
sources: NATO Official Texts, Forbes, Defence24, Al Jazeera, Reuters, AP, The Guardian, NYT, NPR, Financial Express, NBC News, News18, Armenian Weekly, Hurriyet (via News.am), Daily Sabah, Middle East Eye, i24News, Al Hurra, OilPrice, MarineLink, Euro2day, MSN (FT syndicated), CNN
